protocol: medical - supraventricular tachycardia (including atrial fibrillation) medical - tachycardia medical - ventricular tachycardia with a pulse (protocol 2 - 8)

dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a male experiencing breathing difficulty at Patient First.
Hi, I'm Lucas, an EMT. We were told you've been having trouble breathing today. Can you confirm that, sir?
This is a non-emergent interfacility transfer for further evaluation at [COMPANY_NAME].
Yeah, I've been short of breath when I exert-like walking more than a block or two. It passes when I rest.
Sir, I need to check how alert you are. Can you tell me your name and today's date for me? That would help with the exam.
My name's Derrick. October 11th, I believe. Not sure on the date, but [CITY] is the city I'm in.
That's right, we are in [CITY]. You are alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. I'll note you as alert and oriented 4/4.
Mr. Derrick, you mentioned experiencing shortness of breath when you exerted yourself. Was there a specific activity or event leading up to it?

I was walking home from a store, went maybe a block before I started coughing and then I felt the shortness of breath kick in. I sat for a bit, felt better, and then I called a shuttle to bring me here later that day.
You're oriented, answering well, and talking clearly. I'll note you as alert and oriented with no signs of confusion or disorientation.
We're going to check your airway to ensure it's clear, okay Mr. Derrick? I need you to stay still and breathe normally for a moment while I examine it.
Yeah. It's been open, I can swallow and speak just fine, so no obstruction on my end.
Perfect. I'm going to check your breathing next. Can you breathe in deeply and then slowly out for me?
Got it, here I go-
Breathing is normal in depth and rate. Bilateral breath sounds are clear and equal.
Now, we're going to check your pulse and skin condition. Is that okay with you, Mr. Derrick?
Yep, go ahead.
Radial pulse is 63, strong but irregular. Skin is warm, dry, and normal in color. No signs of abnormal skin pigmentation. BP is 141/95.

We performed a 4-lead EKG to monitor your heart rhythm. We'll follow that up with a 12-lead EKG soon to get a more detailed look. The 12-lead EKG interpreted it as showing atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response.
SpO2 at 94%, slightly below normal but not alarming at this time.
BP is 141 over 95. It's elevated systolic but diastolic is in the normal range.
Heart rate is at 63 beats per minute. That is a bit on the slow side, but you aren't showing signs of acute distress.
Respiration rate is 18, within normal limits. No labored breathing or unequal chest expansion. Your breathing seems stable at rest.
Okay, we're going to do a quick blood glucose test now. Mr. Derrick, this will be a small finger prick. You okay with that?
Yeah, go ahead. I've had my blood sugar run high already so I imagine it showed up high on the prior thing for a 300 or so.
Blood glucose is very elevated at 365 mg/dL. This is consistent with diabetes, as you are insulin-dependent.
End-tidal CO2 is currently at 37 with a square waveform. We are monitoring it as part of continuous vital checks.
When you first came into the clinic this morning, what triggered the need for help?

I started coughing, then had trouble breathing. Thought it was a standard cold, but when I walked a block or two, it got worse. Then I called the shuttle and came to Patient First where they did some other tests before you showed up.
Did you have any nausea, or lightheadedness when this happened?
No, it was a breath-related problem. It almost felt caused by coughing and pretty much disappeared once I sat down and took a few deep breaths.
Mr. Derrick, can you tell me about any medical conditions or history of diseases in your background that you've ever been told needed monitoring?
I have diabetes. I'm insulin-dependent, so that needs monitoring-but I haven't had any episodes or attacks before, I'm usually stable.
Mr. Derrick, we will listen to your lungs. You may be asked to take a deep breath in each step. Would you mind allowing this examination?
Yeah, go ahead.
Equal and clear bilateral breath sounds. No adventitious or abnormal lung sounds.
Mr. Derrick's GCS score is 15, indicating he is fully alert and oriented. The score is composed of 4 in Eye, 5 in Verbal, and 6 in Motor. No signs of altered consciousness so far.
Skin appears normal in appearance with no petechia, rashes, or signs of trauma. No signs of jaundice or abnormal turgor.
We placed an 18g IV in your left AC vein for hydration and monitoring while on the way to the hospital.
Normal saline was infused via IV for hydration, given your high blood glucose level. Mr. Derrick's response was stable, and no adverse reactions were noted.
We performed a stroke scale assessment, Mr. Derrick. He showed no signs or symptoms of stroke.
Mr. Derrick reported no pain at this time. His pain score is 0.
Based on the clinical findings, I've decided to follow protocols related to supraventricular tachycardia including atrial fibrillation and ventricular tachycardia for monitoring.
The plan is to transport Mr. Derrick to [COMPANY_NAME] for further cardiology evaluation.
Mr. Derrick, we are going to move you onto the stretcher now. Your left arm with the IV will be supported, and we will keep you secured for transport. You okay with that, sir?
Yep, that's fine with me.
I'll move Mr. Derrick to the stretcher where he'll be secured with a 5-point harness.
We arrived at [COMPANY_NAME]. I will confirm the patient is secured and stable for handoff.
We handed over to the hospital staff, including his stable condition, elevated blood glucose, and findings from the EKG during transport.

The 12-lead EKG showed atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response. Blood glucose was at 365 mg/dL. Vitals at 21:21 documented a pulse of 63, respiratory rate of 18, BP of 141/95, and SpO2 of 94%. Later, at 36:38, he had a pulse of 125, respiratory rate of 15, BP of 172/89, and SpO2 of 95%.
Mr. Derrick is currently pain-free and in no observable distress. No chest pain noted during transport.
Final vitals show pulse of 125, respiratory rate of 15, BP of 172/89, SpO2 of 95%, and EKG showing atrial fibrillation.
That concludes the call and transfer. Have a safe and successful admission, Mr. Derrick.
We'll be clearing the scene now and returning for another shift. Safe travels, everyone!
